changeLog: Removed the intensifier "far" from the dek and from the final takeaway paragraph, since the briefing does not characterize the reduction as 'far' greater—only quantifies it as 37.2%.
Replaced 'carefully designed trial' with 'preregistered randomized controlled trial' to remove unsupported editorializing and match the briefing's own methodological description.
Removed the phrase 'offers encouraging evidence' before the limitations section, replacing with neutral framing to avoid promotional tone.

Added three limitations from the briefing that were omitted from the original draft: the practical/staffing cost of the adaptive schedule's software and tracking requirements, the lack of trainee blinding and possible expectation effects, and the caution that the confidence-performance correlation (r=0.31) means self-reported confidence should not be treated as a proxy for skill.
Expanded the limitations array from 3 to 6 items to match all limitations in the briefing, stated in plain beginner-friendly language, ensuring no limitation is buried or omitted.
Added corresponding bullet points to the 'What this doesn't tell us' section in the body so the body text stays consistent with the expanded limitations list.

claims.claim: Spacing out practice sessions paired with quizzing yourself led to fewer mistakes six months later than cramming did, in nursing students learning a hands-on medical skill.
claims.evidence: coreFinding: 'An adaptive spaced-retrieval schedule improved long-term retention of a procedural nursing skill (sterile catheter insertion)... reducing procedural errors... at 26 weeks.' Spaced retrieval defined as 'practice distributed over expanding intervals with active recall.'

claims.claim: Skills that involve both thinking and doing tend to fade quickly after the initial lesson unless you keep practicing.
claims.evidence: context: 'Procedural skills decay quickly after initial training.' Methods describe 'complex motor-cognitive procedures.'

claims.claim: Massed practice is training packed into a few long sessions, close together in time.
claims.evidence: terminology: 'Massed practice: training concentrated into few, long sessions.'

claims.claim: Spaced retrieval is practice sessions spread across expanding gaps of time, where you actively try to recall and perform the skill.
claims.evidence: terminology: 'Spaced retrieval: practice distributed over expanding intervals with active recall.'

claims.claim: The spacing was made adaptive—a scheduling algorithm adjusted the gap between sessions (2 to 21 days) based on how many mistakes each trainee was making; struggling brought the next session sooner, doing well let it wait longer.
claims.evidence: methods: 'Adaptive-spacing arm... used a scheduling algorithm that expanded or contracted inter-session intervals (2 to 21 days) based on trainee error profiles.'

claims.claim: The trial was a preregistered randomized controlled trial with 412 second-year nursing students at two teaching hospitals over the first half of 2025.
claims.evidence: methods: 'Preregistered randomized controlled trial enrolling 412 second-year nursing students across two sites (January–May 2025)'; limitations note 'Both sites were university-affiliated teaching hospitals.'

claims.claim: The skill was placing a sterile urinary catheter, a procedure with 43 separate steps that need to be done correctly and in the right order.
claims.evidence: coreFinding: 'a procedural nursing skill (sterile catheter insertion)'; methods: '43-step validated checklist.' The draft says 'urinary catheter' — briefing says 'sterile catheter insertion'; dek elsewhere confirms catheter. The 'urinary' specification is a minor addition but broadly consistent.

claims.claim: The procedure's 43 steps need to be done in the right order.
claims.evidence: The briefing describes a '43-step validated checklist' and 'checklist-step retention' as steps 'performed correctly without prompting,' but nowhere states the steps must be performed in a specific order. The ordering requirement is not in the briefing.

claims.claim: Students were randomly split into two equal-sized groups of 206 each.
claims.evidence: methods: 'Adaptive-spacing arm (n=206)... control arm (n=206).'

claims.claim: The control group followed an initial block of training plus two refresher sessions at week 8 and week 16, the massed pattern.
claims.evidence: methods: 'control arm... received standard curriculum (initial block course plus two massed refreshers at weeks 8 and 16).'

claims.claim: Both groups received exactly the same total amount of teaching content.
claims.evidence: methods: 'Both arms received identical total instructional content.'

claims.claim: Six months later (26 weeks), trained assessors who didn't know which group each student was in watched videos and scored errors using the 43-step checklist.
claims.evidence: methods: 'Primary outcome was procedural error rate on a 43-step validated checklist scored by two blinded assessors from video at 26 weeks.'

claims.claim: The spaced group made about 3.9 mistakes per procedure, compared to about 6.2 in the cramming group.
claims.evidence: keyResults: '3.9 errors per procedure versus 6.2 in the massed group.'

claims.claim: This amounted to a 37.2% relative reduction in errors (95% CI, 28.4% to 45.1%; p < 0.001).
claims.evidence: keyResults: 'Relative reduction in procedural error rates: 37.2% (95% CI, 28.4% to 45.1%; p < 0.001).'

claims.claim: The 95% CI and p-value are 'a way of saying researchers are confident this difference is real and not just chance.'
claims.evidence: This is a plain-language gloss on statistical significance (p < 0.001) consistent with the briefing figures; it does not overstate. Acceptable audience-level restatement.

claims.claim: The spaced group correctly remembered about 85 out of every 100 checklist steps (84.6%), compared to about 61 out of 100 (61.3%) for the cramming group.
claims.evidence: keyResults: 'Checklist-step retention at 26 weeks: 84.6% in the adaptive arm versus 61.3% in the control arm.' Rounded restatement preserved with exact figures noted.

claims.claim: The advantage was especially large for steps requiring more physical coordination and skill: spaced group retained about 80 out of 100 (79.8%) vs about 49 out of 100 (48.9%).
claims.evidence: keyResults: 'Retention advantage for high motor complexity steps: 79.8% of steps retained in adaptive arm versus 48.9% in controls.'

claims.claim: Students in the spaced group rated their confidence higher (7.9 out of 10, versus 6.8 out of 10).
claims.evidence: keyResults: 'Trainee-reported confidence at 26 weeks: mean 7.9 vs 6.8 on a 10-point scale.'

claims.claim: How confident students felt only loosely matched how well they actually performed (correlated at r = 0.31).
claims.evidence: keyResults: 'Correlation between confidence and objective performance: r = 0.31.' Limitations: 'The confidence-performance correlation of 0.31 cautions against using self-report as a proxy for competence.'

claims.claim: The spaced-retrieval group spent a median of 11.4 hours in total training, compared to 9.8 hours for the cramming group—about a 16.3% increase.
claims.evidence: keyResults: 'Median cumulative training time: 11.4 hours in the adaptive arm versus 9.8 hours in the control arm'; 'Training time increase: 16.3% increase.'

claims.claim: Once trained, both groups performed the actual procedure at about the same speed: median 14.2 minutes vs 14.9 minutes, a difference too small to be considered meaningful (p = 0.21).
claims.evidence: keyResults: 'Time-to-completion of the full procedure: median 14.2 vs 14.9 minutes; p = 0.21.'

claims.claim: The study tested only one skill in one profession, so we can't assume it works the same for other skills or jobs.
claims.evidence: limitations: 'The trial evaluated a single procedural skill in a single profession; generalization to other skills or professions is not established.'

claims.claim: Students were only followed for about six months, so we don't know if the benefit lasts a full year or longer.
claims.evidence: limitations: 'The 26-week follow-up... does not speak to retention at one year or beyond.'

claims.claim: Both training sites were well-equipped teaching hospitals with strong simulation facilities, so results might look better than in ordinary settings.
claims.evidence: limitations: 'Both sites were university-affiliated teaching hospitals with strong simulation infrastructure, which may inflate effects relative to typical training environments.'

claims.claim: The adaptive schedule needed special scheduling software and tracking of each student's mistakes, which may be hard to set up with fewer resources, and it took 16.3% more training time, which has real staffing costs.
claims.evidence: limitations: 'The adaptive arm required scheduling software and per-trainee tracking that may be impractical in low-resource settings, and the 16.3% increase in training time carries real staffing costs.'

claims.claim: The video scorers didn't know which group a student was in, but the students themselves did, so we can't rule out that this awareness affected performance.
claims.evidence: limitations: 'Assessors were blinded but trainees necessarily were not, and expectation effects cannot be fully excluded.'

claims.claim: Feeling confident shouldn't be mistaken for being skilled (given r = 0.31).
claims.evidence: limitations: 'The confidence-performance correlation of 0.31 cautions against using self-report as a proxy for competence in either arm.'

claims.claim: Spreading practice out over time, combined with actively testing yourself, appears to help a hands-on skill stick better than cramming—at the cost of a modest amount of extra time.
claims.evidence: coreFinding: adaptive spaced-retrieval 'improved long-term retention... compared with standard massed refresher training... though at the cost of modestly increased training time.'

changeLog: Reworded the dek's 'though it took somewhat longer to complete' to 'though it required more cumulative training time,' since the briefing shows training time increased (11.4 vs 9.8 hours) while time-to-completion of the procedure itself did not differ significantly (14.2 vs 14.9 min, p=0.21) — the original phrasing was ambiguous and could be read as contradicting the time-to-completion result.

Removed the added interpretive clause 'so the comparison isolates the effect of how that content was scheduled, not how much of it there was' — this was an inference about study design not stated in the briefing; left as a plain statement of the fact (identical instructional content).

Removed the characterization 'particularly large' describing the high-motor-complexity retention gap, since the briefing does not itself characterize this gap as larger than others; replaced with neutral wording.
Removed 'large' from the keyTakeaway describing the high-motor-complexity retention advantage, for the same reason (unsupported comparative characterization).

Added the missing limitation 'Assessors were blinded but trainees necessarily were not, and expectation effects cannot be fully excluded' to the top-level limitations array — it was present in the body's 'What this doesn't tell us' section and in the briefing but had been dropped from the limitations field, creating an internal inconsistency.

claims.claim: An algorithm-driven spaced-practice schedule beat standard massed refresher training for retaining a clinical procedure six months later
claims.evidence: coreFinding: 'An adaptive spaced-retrieval schedule improved long-term retention of a procedural nursing skill... compared with standard massed refresher training'; primary outcome at 26 weeks (~six months).

claims.claim: It required more cumulative training time
claims.evidence: coreFinding: 'though at the cost of modestly increased training time'; 11.4 vs 9.8 hours, 16.3% increase.

claims.claim: Spacing out practice sessions cuts procedural errors in nursing trainees
claims.evidence: keyResults: 'Adaptive-spacing group committed fewer errors per procedure at 26-week assessment' with 37.2% relative reduction.

claims.claim: Procedural skills tend to decay quickly after initial training
claims.evidence: context: 'Procedural skills decay quickly after initial training.'

claims.claim: Laboratory research has long suggested spacing helps people retain facts and information over time
claims.evidence: context: 'Prior laboratory work suggests spacing improves retention of declarative knowledge.'

claims.claim: Whether the same benefit holds for complex motor-cognitive procedures in real clinical training has been much less studied
claims.evidence: context: 'evidence for complex motor-cognitive procedures in real clinical training has been limited.'

claims.claim: Most prior work relied on fixed practice schedules rather than adaptive ones
claims.evidence: context: 'most studies used fixed rather than adaptive schedules.'

claims.claim: Massed practice: training concentrated into a few long sessions
claims.evidence: terminology: 'Massed practice': 'training concentrated into few, long sessions'.

claims.claim: Spaced retrieval: practice spread out over expanding intervals, with active recall built in
claims.evidence: terminology: 'Spaced retrieval': 'practice distributed over expanding intervals with active recall'.

claims.claim: Checklist-step retention: percentage of validated procedure steps performed correctly without prompting
claims.evidence: terminology: 'Checklist-step retention': 'percentage of validated procedure steps performed correctly without prompting'.

claims.claim: Preregistered RCT with 412 second-year nursing students across two sites, enrolled January–May 2025
claims.evidence: methods: 'Preregistered randomized controlled trial enrolling 412 second-year nursing students across two sites (January–May 2025).'

claims.claim: Students were stratified by site and baseline skill score, split into two groups of 206 each
claims.evidence: methods: 'stratified by site and baseline skill score. Adaptive-spacing arm (n=206)... control arm (n=206).'

claims.claim: Adaptive arm scheduled by an algorithm that expanded or contracted the gap between sessions (from 2 to 21 days) based on error patterns
claims.evidence: methods: 'a scheduling algorithm that expanded or contracted inter-session intervals (2 to 21 days) based on trainee error profiles.'

claims.claim: Control arm: initial block course plus two massed refresher sessions at weeks 8 and 16
claims.evidence: methods: 'standard curriculum (initial block course plus two massed refreshers at weeks 8 and 16).'

claims.claim: Both groups received identical total instructional content
claims.evidence: methods: 'Both arms received identical total instructional content.'

claims.claim: Main outcome — procedural error rate on validated 43-step checklist — scored at 26 weeks from video by two blinded assessors (kappa=0.87)
claims.evidence: methods: 'Primary outcome was procedural error rate on a 43-step validated checklist scored by two blinded assessors from video at 26 weeks (inter-rater reliability kappa=0.87).'

claims.claim: 3.9 errors per procedure in adaptive arm versus 6.2 in massed control arm at 26 weeks
claims.evidence: keyResults figure: '3.9 errors per procedure versus 6.2 in the massed group.'

claims.claim: 37.2% relative reduction in procedural error rates (95% CI, 28.4% to 45.1%; p < 0.001)
84.6% of checklist steps retained in adaptive arm versus 61.3% in control at 26 weeks
claims.evidence: keyResults figure: '84.6% in the adaptive arm versus 61.3% in the control arm.'

claims.claim: High motor-complexity steps showed retention advantage: 79.8% adaptive versus 48.9% control
claims.evidence: keyResults figure: '79.8% of steps retained in adaptive arm versus 48.9% in controls.'

claims.claim: Time-to-completion did not differ meaningfully: median 14.2 vs 14.9 minutes (p = 0.21)
claims.evidence: keyResults figure: 'median 14.2 vs 14.9 minutes; p = 0.21.'

claims.claim: Trainee-reported confidence at 26 weeks higher in adaptive arm: mean 7.9 vs 6.8 on 10-point scale
claims.evidence: keyResults figure: 'mean 7.9 vs 6.8 on a 10-point scale.'

claims.claim: Correlation between self-reported confidence and objective scores was r = 0.31, meaning confidence isn't a reliable stand-in for competence
claims.evidence: keyResults 'r = 0.31'; limitations: 'confidence-performance correlation of 0.31 cautions against using self-report as a proxy for competence.'

claims.claim: Adaptive arm required median 11.4 hours cumulative training time versus 9.8 hours control — a 16.3% increase
claims.evidence: keyResults figures: '11.4 hours in the adaptive arm versus 9.8 hours' and '16.3% increase.'

claims.claim: Tested one procedural skill (sterile catheter insertion) in one profession (nursing), so generalization not established
claims.evidence: coreFinding names 'sterile catheter insertion'; limitations: 'a single procedural skill in a single profession; generalization... is not established.'

claims.claim: Follow-up ran to 26 weeks — longer than much prior work, but says nothing about retention at one year or beyond
claims.evidence: limitations: 'The 26-week follow-up, while longer than most prior studies, does not speak to retention at one year or beyond.'

claims.claim: Adaptive schedule depended on scheduling software and per-trainee tracking, may be hard where resources limited; 16.3% time increase has staffing cost implications
claims.evidence: limitations: 'required scheduling software and per-trainee tracking that may be impractical in low-resource settings, and the 16.3% increase in training time carries real staffing costs.'

claims.claim: Assessors blinded, trainees not, so expectation effects can't be fully ruled out
claims.evidence: limitations: 'Assessors were blinded but trainees necessarily were not, and expectation effects cannot be fully excluded.'

claims.claim: Both sites were university-affiliated teaching hospitals with strong simulation resources, which may inflate effects vs typical settings
claims.evidence: limitations: 'Both sites were university-affiliated teaching hospitals with strong simulation infrastructure, which may inflate effects relative to typical training environments.'

claims.claim: Untested whether adaptive spacing beats a well-designed fixed spacing schedule; comparison was against massed refreshers
claims.evidence: openQuestions: 'Whether adaptive spacing outperforms well-designed fixed spacing (rather than massed refreshers) remains untested.'

claims.claim: How much retention gain is worth the added training time hasn't been modeled
claims.evidence: openQuestions: 'The cost-effectiveness threshold at which the retention gain justifies the added training time is unmodeled.'

claims.claim: How the algorithm performs when early error data is sparse hasn't been modeled
claims.evidence: openQuestions: 'The algorithm's sensitivity to sparse early error data is unmodeled.'

changeLog: Title: changed '37%' to the briefing's exact figure '37.2%' — style guide requires quoting figures exactly without rounding.

Interpretation section: removed the sentence claiming the design 'supports attributing the effect to the scheduling manipulation rather than to differences in instructional content or assessor bias' — this causal-attribution inference is not made in the briefing and exceeds it.

Design and methods section: removed the interpretive phrase 'isolating scheduling as the manipulated variable' and replaced with a plain factual statement, since this framing asserts an inferential conclusion not stated in the briefing.

Background section: removed 'long' from 'Laboratory research has long suggested' — the briefing does not characterize the historical duration of this evidence, only that 'prior laboratory work suggests' it.

Limitations: removed the added seventh bullet that merged the three briefing 'openQuestions' into the limitations list — the briefing treats these as separate open questions, not limitations, and they are already presented correctly in the body's Open Questions section; including them as limitations mischaracterized their status and duplicated content.

claims.claim: Adaptive spaced retrieval cuts procedural errors by 37.2% at 26 weeks in a nursing RCT
claims.evidence: keyResults: 'Relative reduction in procedural error rates' figure '37.2% (95% CI, 28.4% to 45.1%; p < 0.001)'; coreFinding confirms 26-week reduction.

claims.claim: The trial was preregistered and concerned sterile catheter insertion training
claims.evidence: methods: 'Preregistered randomized controlled trial'; coreFinding: 'a procedural nursing skill (sterile catheter insertion)'.

claims.claim: An algorithm-driven, expanding-interval spacing schedule outperforms standard massed refreshers on long-term error rate and checklist retention, at a modest cost in training time
claims.evidence: coreFinding: 'improved long-term retention... compared with standard massed refresher training, reducing procedural errors by 37.2% and improving checklist-step retention to 84.6%... though at the cost of modestly increased training time.'

claims.claim: Procedural skills decay quickly after initial training
claims.evidence: context: 'Procedural skills decay quickly after initial training.'

claims.claim: Laboratory research suggested spacing improves retention relative to massed practice, but the evidence base is concentrated in declarative knowledge and fixed rather than adaptive schedules
claims.evidence: context: 'Prior laboratory work suggests spacing improves retention of declarative knowledge... most studies used fixed rather than adaptive schedules.'

claims.claim: Evidence for complex motor-cognitive procedures in real clinical training contexts has been limited
claims.evidence: context: 'evidence for complex motor-cognitive procedures in real clinical training has been limited.'

claims.claim: The RCT enrolled 412 second-year nursing students across two sites, January–May 2025, stratified by site and baseline skill score
claims.evidence: methods: 'enrolling 412 second-year nursing students across two sites (January–May 2025), stratified by site and baseline skill score.'

claims.claim: Adaptive-spacing arm (n=206) used a scheduling algorithm expanding or contracting inter-session intervals (2 to 21 days) based on trainee error profiles
claims.evidence: methods: 'Adaptive-spacing arm (n=206) used a scheduling algorithm that expanded or contracted inter-session intervals (2 to 21 days) based on trainee error profiles.'

claims.claim: Control arm (n=206) received standard curriculum: initial block course plus two massed refreshers at weeks 8 and 16
claims.evidence: methods: 'control arm (n=206) received standard curriculum (initial block course plus two massed refreshers at weeks 8 and 16).'

claims.claim: Both arms received identical total instructional content
claims.evidence: methods: 'Both arms received identical total instructional content.'

claims.claim: Primary outcome was procedural error rate on a 43-step validated checklist, scored by two blinded assessors from video at 26 weeks, with kappa=0.87
claims.evidence: methods: 'Primary outcome was procedural error rate on a 43-step validated checklist scored by two blinded assessors from video at 26 weeks (inter-rater reliability kappa=0.87).'

claims.claim: Secondary outcomes included checklist-step retention, time-to-completion, and trainee-reported confidence
claims.evidence: methods: 'Secondary outcomes included checklist-step retention, time-to-completion, and trainee-reported confidence.'

claims.claim: At 26 weeks, the adaptive-spacing group committed 3.9 errors per procedure versus 6.2 in the massed control group
claims.evidence: keyResults: '3.9 errors per procedure versus 6.2 in the massed group'.

claims.claim: Relative reduction in procedural error rate of 37.2% (95% CI, 28.4% to 45.1%; p < 0.001)
Overall checklist-step retention at 26 weeks was 84.6% in the adaptive arm versus 61.3% in the control arm
claims.evidence: keyResults: '84.6% in the adaptive arm versus 61.3% in the control arm'.

claims.claim: For high motor-complexity steps, 79.8% retained in the adaptive arm versus 48.9% in controls
claims.evidence: keyResults: 'Retention advantage for high motor complexity steps' figure '79.8% of steps retained in adaptive arm versus 48.9% in controls'.

claims.claim: Median cumulative training time was 11.4 hours in the adaptive arm versus 9.8 hours in the control arm — a 16.3% increase
claims.evidence: keyResults: '11.4 hours in the adaptive arm versus 9.8 hours in the control arm'; 'Training time increase' figure '16.3% increase'.

claims.claim: Time-to-completion of the full procedure: median 14.2 vs 14.9 minutes; p = 0.21 (no statistically significant difference)
claims.evidence: keyResults: 'median 14.2 vs 14.9 minutes; p = 0.21'.

claims.claim: Trainee-reported confidence at 26 weeks was mean 7.9 vs 6.8 on a 10-point scale
claims.evidence: keyResults: 'mean 7.9 vs 6.8 on a 10-point scale'.

claims.claim: Confidence-performance correlation was r = 0.31, a modest association not supporting self-reported confidence as a proxy for objectively scored competence in either arm
claims.evidence: keyResults: 'Correlation between confidence and objective performance' r = 0.31; limitations: 'The confidence-performance correlation of 0.31 cautions against using self-report as a proxy for competence in either arm.'

claims.claim: Trainees in the adaptive arm felt more confident and performed better, but confidence tracked actual performance only loosely, in both groups
claims.evidence: Supported by confidence figures (7.9 vs 6.8), performance figures (fewer errors, higher retention), and limitations note on r=0.31 cautioning against self-report as proxy 'in either arm.'

claims.claim: The trial evaluated a single procedural skill in a single profession; generalization to other skills or professions is not established
claims.evidence: limitations: 'The trial evaluated a single procedural skill in a single profession; generalization to other skills or professions is not established.'

claims.claim: The 26-week follow-up exceeds most prior studies but does not address retention at one year or beyond
claims.evidence: limitations: 'The 26-week follow-up, while longer than most prior studies, does not speak to retention at one year or beyond.'

claims.claim: The adaptive arm required scheduling software and per-trainee tracking that may be impractical in low-resource settings, and the 16.3% time increase carries real staffing costs
claims.evidence: limitations: 'The adaptive arm required scheduling software and per-trainee tracking that may be impractical in low-resource settings, and the 16.3% increase in training time carries real staffing costs.'

claims.claim: Trainees were not and could not be blinded; assessor blinding does not eliminate the possibility of expectation effects on trainee behavior
claims.evidence: limitations: 'Assessors were blinded but trainees necessarily were not, and expectation effects cannot be fully excluded.'

claims.claim: Both sites were university-affiliated teaching hospitals with strong simulation infrastructure, which may inflate the observed effect relative to typical training environments
claims.evidence: limitations: 'Both sites were university-affiliated teaching hospitals with strong simulation infrastructure, which may inflate effects relative to typical training environments.'

claims.claim: Whether adaptive spacing outperforms well-designed fixed spacing schedules (as opposed to massed refreshers) remains untested by this trial
claims.evidence: openQuestions: 'Whether adaptive spacing outperforms well-designed fixed spacing (rather than massed refreshers) remains untested.'

claims.claim: The cost-effectiveness threshold at which the retention gain justifies the added training time is unmodeled
claims.evidence: openQuestions: 'The cost-effectiveness threshold at which the retention gain justifies the added training time is unmodeled.'

claims.claim: The algorithm's sensitivity to sparse early error data (behavior when initial trainee performance data are limited) is unmodeled
claims.evidence: openQuestions: 'The algorithm's sensitivity to sparse early error data is unmodeled.'

claims.claim: The arms were content-matched, differing only in scheduling
claims.evidence: methods: 'Both arms received identical total instructional content'; arms differ in scheduling algorithm vs standard curriculum, consistent with 'differing only in scheduling.'

claims.claim: Massed practice is training concentrated into few, long sessions; spaced retrieval is practice distributed over expanding intervals with active recall
claims.evidence: terminology: 'Massed practice: training concentrated into few, long sessions'; 'Spaced retrieval: practice distributed over expanding intervals with active recall.'
